title: A small change that improved your week
summary: Narrate one small habit or decision you made that had a noticeably positive effect, and explain why it worked.
topic: A small change that improved your week
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (350–500 words) narrating a small change you made — or could make — to your daily routine, and explain the effect it had. Be specific about the change and its results. Use present perfect, modal verbs, and at least one sentence with 'although' or 'however'.
hintStarters: The change itself was almost embarrassingly small…
What surprised me was not the change but…
Although I had expected to feel different after a week, the actual result was…

hintVocab.phrase: to get a full night's sleep

hintVocab.meaningVi: cố ý nỗ lực có chủ đích

sampleText:
Three months ago I stopped checking my phone for the first thirty minutes after waking up. That was the whole change — nothing dramatic, nothing expensive. I had read about the idea somewhere and had ignored it for years, assuming it was the kind of advice that sounds reasonable but makes no real difference. The first morning was harder than I expected. I lay there noticing how much I wanted to pick up the phone, which told me more about the habit than any article had.

What surprised me was not the change itself but the ripple effect it had on the rest of the morning. Although I had expected to feel different after a week, the actual result came faster: by the third day, I was eating breakfast without reading anything, which meant I was finishing it while it was still warm. I started arriving at work with a clearer head, not because I had done anything impressive but simply because I hadn't spent the first hour absorbing other people's urgency. The mornings felt longer without being any longer. I have since extended the rule to the last thirty minutes before sleep, which has made the same kind of quiet difference.

However, the change has not been perfectly smooth. There have been mornings when I picked the phone up anyway, usually because I was waiting for something important, and on those days I could feel the old rhythm returning immediately — a slight restlessness, a sense that the morning had already slipped away. That told me the change was real. Small habits are not small because their effects are small; they are small because they are easy to start and easy to undo. The difficult part is not making the change but noticing when you have drifted back without realising it. Three months in, I still have to make a conscious effort on difficult mornings, but it has stopped feeling like discipline and started feeling like preference, which is probably the point at which a habit has actually taken hold.

sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: hấp thụ sự cấp bách của người khác

sampleAnnotations.phrase: The mornings felt longer without being any longer
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: Buổi sáng cảm thấy dài hơn mà không thực sự dài hơn

sampleAnnotations.phrase: I have since extended the rule
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: Kể từ đó tôi đã mở rộng quy tắc
nhịp điệu cũ quay trở lại

sampleAnnotations.phrase: easy to start and easy to undo
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: dễ bắt đầu và dễ phá bỏ
trôi dạt về lại mà không nhận ra
cố ý nỗ lực có chủ đích
ngừng cảm thấy như kỷ luật
bắt đầu cảm thấy như sở thích

sampleAnnotations.phrase: a habit has actually taken hold
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: một thói quen đã thực sự bám rễ

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Present perfect for recent and ongoing results
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'I have since extended the rule to the last thirty minutes before sleep' dùng hiện tại hoàn thành để cho thấy một hành động bắt đầu trong quá khứ vẫn đang có hiệu lực ở hiện tại — 'since' nhấn mạnh sự liên tục từ một thời điểm nhất định.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'I have since extended the rule to the last thirty minutes before sleep' uses the present perfect to show an action begun in the past that is still in effect now — 'since' emphasises continuity from a specific point.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Concession with 'although' before a surprising result
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Although I had expected to feel different after a week, the actual result came faster' — 'although' dùng để đặt ra một kỳ vọng trước khi tiết lộ một kết quả bất ngờ. Lưu ý 'had expected' dùng quá khứ hoàn thành vì hành động xảy ra trước thời điểm được đề cập trong câu.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Although I had expected to feel different after a week, the actual result came faster' — 'although' sets up an expectation before revealing a surprising outcome. Note 'had expected' uses past perfect because the expectation came before the result being described.

sampleTranslationVi:
Ba tháng trước tôi ngừng kiểm tra điện thoại trong ba mươi phút đầu sau khi thức dậy. Đó là toàn bộ sự thay đổi — không có gì kịch tính, không tốn kém. Tôi đã đọc về ý tưởng này ở đâu đó và đã bỏ qua nó nhiều năm, cho rằng đó là loại lời khuyên nghe có vẻ hợp lý nhưng không tạo ra sự khác biệt thực sự. Buổi sáng đầu tiên khó hơn tôi mong đợi. Tôi nằm đó nhận thấy mình muốn cầm điện thoại lên nhiều đến mức nào, điều đó cho tôi biết nhiều hơn về thói quen này so với bất kỳ bài viết nào.

Điều làm tôi ngạc nhiên không phải là bản thân sự thay đổi mà là hiệu ứng lan tỏa của nó đối với phần còn lại của buổi sáng. Mặc dù tôi đã kỳ vọng cảm thấy khác sau một tuần, kết quả thực tế đến nhanh hơn: đến ngày thứ ba, tôi đang ăn sáng mà không đọc gì, điều đó có nghĩa là tôi ăn xong khi thức ăn vẫn còn nóng. Tôi bắt đầu đến nơi làm việc với đầu óc minh mẫn hơn, không phải vì tôi đã làm gì ấn tượng mà đơn giản là vì tôi chưa dành giờ đầu tiên hấp thụ sự cấp bách của người khác. Buổi sáng cảm thấy dài hơn mà không thực sự dài hơn. Kể từ đó tôi đã mở rộng quy tắc này cho ba mươi phút cuối trước khi ngủ, điều này cũng tạo ra loại sự khác biệt yên tĩnh tương tự.

Tuy nhiên, sự thay đổi không hoàn toàn suôn sẻ. Có những buổi sáng tôi vẫn cầm điện thoại lên, thường là vì tôi đang chờ thứ gì đó quan trọng, và vào những ngày đó tôi có thể cảm nhận nhịp điệu cũ quay trở lại ngay lập tức — một chút bồn chồn, cảm giác buổi sáng đã trôi qua mất rồi. Điều đó cho tôi thấy sự thay đổi là có thực. Những thói quen nhỏ không nhỏ vì tác động của chúng nhỏ; chúng nhỏ vì dễ bắt đầu và dễ phá bỏ. Phần khó khăn không phải là thực hiện sự thay đổi mà là nhận ra khi bạn đã trôi dạt về lại mà không nhận ra. Ba tháng sau, tôi vẫn phải cố ý nỗ lực có chủ đích vào những buổi sáng khó khăn, nhưng nó đã ngừng cảm thấy như kỷ luật và bắt đầu cảm thấy như sở thích, đó có lẽ là thời điểm một thói quen đã thực sự bám rễ.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the present perfect correctly to describe a continuing result?
options: I have since extend the rule to the last thirty minutes before sleep.
I since extended the rule to the last thirty minutes before sleep.
I have since extended the rule to the last thirty minutes before sleep.
I am since extending the rule to the last thirty minutes before sleep.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present perfect = have/has + past participle; 'extended' is the past participle of 'extend'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Have + past participle' for an action with a continuing present effect.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'although' correctly to introduce an unexpected result?
options: Although I had expected to feel different after a week, but the actual result came faster.
Although I had expected to feel different after a week, the actual result came faster, however.
I had expected to feel different after a week, although the actual result came faster.
Although I had expected to feel different after a week, the actual result came faster.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Although + concession clause, main clause' — no extra 'but'; the concession clause comes first.
mcQuestions.hint: Put the 'although' clause first; don't add 'but' in the main clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option correctly uses 'however' to introduce a qualification?
options: However, the change has not been perfectly smooth.
However the change has not been perfectly smooth.
The change, however has not been perfectly smooth.
The change has not been perfectly smooth, however the mornings improved.
mcQuestions.explanation: Sentence-initial 'however' must be followed by a comma when used as a discourse marker.
mcQuestions.hint: A sentence-initial 'however' always takes a comma after it.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the past perfect correctly to show sequence?
options: I have read about the idea somewhere and have ignored it for years.
I had read about the idea somewhere and had ignored it for years.
I read about the idea somewhere and had ignored it for years.
I had read about the idea somewhere and ignored it for years ago.
mcQuestions.explanation: Both verbs describe actions completed before the narrative's main time frame, so past perfect is correct for both.
mcQuestions.hint: When two past events both precede the main story, use past perfect for both.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses a modal to describe a past perception?
options: On those days I can feel the old rhythm returning immediately.
On those days I could felt the old rhythm returning immediately.
On those days I could feel the old rhythm returning immediately.
On those days I could to feel the old rhythm returning immediately.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Could + bare infinitive' expresses past ability or perception; no 'to', no past form after 'could'.
mcQuestions.hint: Past ability: 'could + bare infinitive' — no 'to', no '-ed'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which gerund phrase is used correctly as the subject of a clause?
options: The difficult part is not to make the change but noticing when you have drifted back.
The difficult part is not making the change but to notice when you have drifted back.
The difficult part is not make the change but notice when you have drifted back.
The difficult part is not making the change but noticing when you have drifted back.
mcQuestions.explanation: Parallel structure: 'not making … but noticing' — both gerunds are parallel after 'is not … but'.
mcQuestions.hint: After 'not … but', use parallel forms; if one is a gerund, the other should be too.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase correctly expresses a subtle emotional state?
options: a slight restlessness
a slight restless
a slightly restlessness
a restless slight
mcQuestions.explanation: Adjective + noun: 'slight' (adjective) + 'restlessness' (noun); 'slightly' would modify an adjective, not be used before a noun.
mcQuestions.hint: Use an adjective (not adverb) directly before a noun.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'stopped + gerund' vs 'started + gerund'?
options: It stopped to feel like discipline and started to feel like preference.
It stopped feeling like discipline and started feeling like preference.
It stopped feeling like discipline and started to feel like preference.
It stopped to feel like discipline and started feeling like preference.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Stop + gerund' and 'start + gerund' are both correct; maintain parallel form within the same sentence.
mcQuestions.hint: Keep both verbs parallel: both gerunds or both infinitives.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence best uses a relative clause to add information?
options: I was waiting for something important, that meant the morning had already started badly.
I was waiting for something important, which it meant the morning had already started badly.
I was waiting for something important, which meant the morning had already started badly.
I was waiting for something important, what meant the morning had already started badly.
mcQuestions.explanation: Non-defining relative clauses referring to an entire clause use 'which', not 'that', and don't repeat the subject.
mcQuestions.hint: Use 'which' (not 'that') to refer back to an entire clause or idea.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option correctly places an adverb of degree?
options: I was eating breakfast without reading anything, which meant I was finishing it while it still was warm.
I was eating breakfast without reading nothing, which meant I was finishing it while it was still warm.
I was eating breakfast without to read anything, which meant I was finishing it while it was still warm.
I was eating breakfast without reading anything, which meant I was finishing it while it was still warm.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Still' is placed before the adjective 'warm' it modifies; 'without + gerund', not 'without + to infinitive'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Still' goes before the adjective it modifies; 'without' takes a gerund.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence best uses a B1 introductory phrase to signal a contrast?
options: What surprised me was not the change itself but the ripple effect it had.
What surprising me was not the change itself but the ripple effect it had.
What surprised me was not the change itself but the ripple effect it was having.
What surprised me was not the change itself but the ripple effect which it had.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'What surprised me was…' is a cleft sentence structure used to highlight contrast; 'surprised' is past simple, not a participle.
mcQuestions.hint: Cleft sentences: 'What + past simple + was…' focus attention on the new information.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Why do small changes sometimes produce bigger effects than large ones? Can you think of an example where a large, dramatic change had less impact than expected?
